content.full_text:
RAW MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 7, 1997
R. Zachary Wasserman
Wendel, Rosen, Black & Dean LLP
1111 Broadway, 24
th Floor
Post Office Box 2047
Oakland, California 94604-2047
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-153
Dear Mr. Wasserman:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1. Are members of the expenditure plan development committee (“EPDC”) public
officials subject to the conflict of interest and disclosure provisions of the Act?
2. Are members of the EPDC who are elected officials subject to section 84308’s
prohibition on contributions to members of appointed boards or commissions?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Under the facts you have presented, members of the EPDC are not public officials
who will be making governmental decisions as defined in the Act. Therefore members of the
EPDC are not subject to the conflict-of-interest and economic disclosure provisions of the Act.
2. Under the facts you have described, members of the EPDC will not be conducting
proceedings involving a “license, permit, or other entitlement for use.” Therefore members of
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-153
Page 2
the EPDC are not subject to section 84308’s prohibition on contributions to members of
appointed boards and commissions.
FACTS
You represent the Alameda County Transportation Authority (“ACTA”) and are
requesting advice on issues regarding the newly formed Expenditure Plan Development
Committee which is an advisory committee to an advisory committee.
1. Background Information Regarding ACTA. ACTA is a local government agency
formed pursuant to Public Utility Code section 13100 et. seq. (the Bay Area County Traffic and
Transportation Funding Act) following a vote of approval by Alameda County Voters of
Measure B on the November 1996 ballot. The Authority’s purpose is to collect a one half cent
sales tax and use the proceeds to implement a Transportation Expenditure Plan involving
highway, transit, and local road improvements. The members of the Authority consist of all five
members of the Alameda County Board of Supervisors and four Mayors representing cities in
Alameda County.
2. Expenditure Plan Development Committee. The current Measure B program for
transportation improvements in Alameda County is set to expire in the year 2002. ACTA is
required by law to prepare a new expenditure plan. A Steering Committee ofrime elected
officials representing ACTA and the Alameda County Congestion Management Agency, a joint
powers agency composed ofAlameda County and the fourteen cities in the county, will oversee
the preparation of a new expenditure plan.
The Steering Committee will form the EPDC. The EPDC, a body of community
representatives, will review the projects and programs submitted for future consideration using
criteria established by the Steering Committee. The EPDC will ultimately recommend an
expenditure plan to the Steering Committee and help test the levels ofpublic acceptability and
support for elements of the plan. The Steering Committee will review the recommendation and
may change it. The Steering Committee will then submit the plan to the Congestion
Management Agency and Metropolitan Transportation Commission for comments. After
reviewing and, if appropriate, incorporating the Congestion Management Agency’s and the
Metropolitan Transportation Commission’s comments, the Steering Committee will submit the
expenditure plan to ACTA. ACTA will consider and perhaps change the plan further.
After ACTA approves the plan, it will be submitted to the Board of Supervisors (the
“Board”) and the cities in Alameda County for approval. If the Board and a majority of the cities
representing a majority of the population in the county approves the plan, the Board will place
the plan on the ballot for voter approval. The EPDC will cease to exist once the new expenditure
plan has been approved or rejected.


File No. A-97-153
Page 3
ANALYSIS
1. Conflict -of-Interest and Economic Disclosure. The Act prohibits a public official
from malcing, participating in making, or in any way attempting to influence a governmental
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.) The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to “public officials.”
A public official is defined as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. (Section 82048.) Regulation 18700(a)(1) provides that “member” includes,
but is not limited to, salaried or unsalaried members ofboards or commissions with
decisionmaking authority. For example, the members of a local transportation authority which
has decisionmalcing authority, such as ACTA, are public officials who are subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act. (Fellows Advice Letter, No. 1-91-395.)
Under Regulation 18700(a)(1), a board or commission possesses decisionmaking authority
whenever:
“(A) It may make a final governmental decision;
(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may
prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not
be overriden; or
(C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and
over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved
without significant amendment or modification by another public
official or governmental agency.”
If the EPDC meets any of the tests of Regulation 18700(a)(1)(A), (B) or (C), it possesses
decisionmaking authority, and its members would be considered public officials who must file
statements of economic interests and are subject to the Act’s conflict-of-interest rules.
Alternatively, if the EPDC does not have decisionmaking authority under Regulation
18700(a)(1), the committee’s members are not considered public officials under the Act and are
not subject to the economic disclosure and conflict-of-interest rules solely by virtue of their
membership on the EPDC.
The EPDC will hold a series of public meetings and will listen to advice from technical
consultants and the public about which transportation projects should be included in the
expenditure plan. About 100 project proposals have been submitted by public agencies for
consideration, such as the extension ofBART to the Oakland airport, improvements on the
580/680 corridor, the addition ofhigh-occupancy-vehicle lanes on certain highways, and
operating and capital improvements in the Alameda County Transit commuter rail. Applying the
guidelines set forth by the Steering Committee, the EPDC will recommend to the Steering
Committee which projects should be included in the expenditure plan. You stated that the
Steering Committee will review the recommendation of the EPDC and may change it. The
Steering Committee will then submit the plan to the Congestion Management Agency and the
Metropolitan Transportation Commission for comments. After reviewing and possibly


File No. A-97-153
Page 4
incorporating these comments, the Steering Committee will submit the expenditure plan to
ACTA.
ACTA will consider and perhaps change the plan further. After ACTA approves the
plan, it will be submitted to the Board of Supervisors and the cities in Alameda County for
approval. If the Board and a majority ofthe cities representing a majority ofthe population in
the county approves the plan, the Board will place the plan on the ballot for voter approval. The
EPDC is a newly formed committee whose task is developing and testing public support for a
transit expenditure plan. The committee will cease to exist after the expenditure plan has been
approved or rejected.
According to the facts you have provided, the EPDC is a committee with a solely
advisory function. It does not have the power to make or compel a final governmental decision.
The expenditure plan which it recommends will be subject to review and modification by two
entities — the Steering Committee and ACTA. Further, the EPDC’s substantive
recommendations will not, over an extended period of time, be regularly approved without
significant amendment by another governmental agency, because the EPDC only exists to
develop this transit expenditure plan. Because the EPDC does not have decisionmaking
authority, its members are not considered public officials under the Act. Therefore, they are not
covered by the Act’s economic disclosure and conflict-of-interest disqualification provisions.
You ask two specific conflict of interest questions about whether a member of the EPDC
is prohibited from voting on or participating in discussions about the expenditure plan if one of
the transportation projects will improve access to a development site owned by the member and
if one project will expand an existing project with which the member has a consulting contract.
As discussed above, assuming the EPDC members are acting in a solely advisory capacity, the
disqualification rules of section 87100 do not apply to them.
2. Section 84308. You also ask whether members of the EPDC who are elected officials
are subject to section 84308’s prohibition on contributions to members of appointed boards or
commissions. Section 84308 is designed to ensure that appointed members ofboards or
commissions are not biased by large campaign contributors or potential contributors who might
appear before them in a proceeding involving a license, permit or entitlement for use. Section
84308 applies to all appointed officers of any state agency or local government agency, with the
exception ofthe courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government, the Legislature, the
Board of Equalization, and constitutional officers. (Section 84308(a)(3).)
Section 84308 imposes two requirements on officers subject to the section. First, no
officer of an agency shall accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of more than $250, from any
party, or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, while a proceeding
involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the agency and for
three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding. (Section
84308(b).) Second, if an officer has in fact accepted a contribution ofmore than $250 during the
last 12 months from a party or participant in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other


File No. A-97-153
Page 5
entitlement for use pending before an agency, the officer must disclose that fact on the record of
the proceeding and must disqualify himself or herself from participating. (Section 84308(c).)
“License, permit, or other entitlement for use” is defined to include all business,
professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other entitlements for use, including
all entitlements for land use, all contracts (other than competitively bid, labor, or personal
employment contracts), and all franchises. (Section 84308(a)(5).) Regulation 18438.2(a)
provides that for purposes of Section 84308, a “proceeding involving a license, permit or other
entitlement for use” includes any proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modify a license,
permit or other entitlement for use. (See City ofAgoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation
Commission (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 480.)
According to the facts you have provided, the EPDC is an advisory body that does not
have the power to make governmental decisions. The EPDC will not be making decisions in any
proceedings involving a “license, permit or other entitlement for use.” Under your facts, the
EPDC will not be making any decisions in proceedings involving contracts or entitlements for
land use as it develops the transit expenditure plan. Therefore, the members ofthe EPDC will
not be covered by section 84308.2
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
P.
By: Hyla P. Wagner
-
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:HPW:ak
2 Note that the members of ACTA, including members of the Alameda County Board of Supervisors when
sitting as ex-officio members of ACTA, are subject to the provisions of section 84308. (Washington Advice Letter,
No. 1-91-521 and Hickling Advice Letter, No. A-87-214.) In addition, the Steering Committee members are subject
to the provisions of section 84308.

prohibition on contributions to members of appointed boards or commissions?

2. Under the facts you have described, members of the EPDC will not be conducting
proceedings involving a “license, permit, or other entitlement for use.” Therefore members of428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

the EPDC are not subject to section 84308’s prohibition on contributions to members of
appointed boards and commissions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Are members of the expenditure plan development committee (“EPDC”) public
officials subject to the conflict of interest and disclosure provisions of the Act?
2. Are members of the EPDC who are elected officials subject to section 84308’s
prohibition on contributions to members of appointed boards or commissions?

CONCLUSION: 1. Under the facts you have presented, members of the EPDC are not public officials
who will be making governmental decisions as defined in the Act. Therefore members of the
EPDC are not subject to the conflict-of-interest and economic disclosure provisions of the Act.
2. Under the facts you have described, members of the EPDC will not be conducting
proceedings involving a “license, permit, or other entitlement for use.” Therefore members of428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660
the EPDC are not subject to section 84308’s prohibition on contributions to members of
appointed boards and commissions.
